content.summary: Configure multi-display output in TouchDesigner using Window COMPs. This tutorial covers setting up multiple output windows, positioning them on specific monitors, configuring borderless fullscreen, and synchronizing content across displays for installations and live shows.

sections.title: Understanding Window COMPs
sections.content:
The **Window COMP** is TouchDesigner's gateway to external displays. Each Window COMP can open a separate OS window that can be positioned on any connected monitor. For multi-screen setups, you create one Window COMP per output display.

Each Window COMP has an `Operator` parameter that defines which TOP provides its content. The window renders that TOP's output at the specified resolution and position.

sections.title: Position Windows on Specific Monitors
sections.content:
Use the `Window Placement` parameters to control where each window appears. Set `Borders` to `Off` and `Opening Size` to match your display resolution for seamless fullscreen output.

The `Offset` parameters (`Offset X`, `Offset Y`) position the window in desktop coordinates. Monitor positions can be found in Windows Display Settings — the second monitor might start at x=1920 if it's to the right of a 1080p primary display.
sections.code:
# Configure two Window COMPs for dual-screen output
# Window 1 — primary display
win1 = op('/project1/window1')
win1.par.winoffsetx = 0
win1.par.winoffsety = 0
win1.par.winw = 1920
win1.par.winh = 1080
win1.par.borders = False

# Window 2 — second display (offset by primary width)
win2 = op('/project1/window2')
win2.par.winoffsetx = 1920
win2.par.winoffsety = 0
win2.par.winw = 1920
win2.par.winh = 1080
win2.par.borders = False

sections.title: Detect Monitors Programmatically
sections.content:
TouchDesigner provides the `monitors` object to query connected displays at runtime. This is essential for installations that need to adapt to different hardware setups without manual reconfiguration.

Use `monitors` to auto-position your Window COMPs on the correct displays.
sections.code:
# Query available monitors
for i, m in enumerate(monitors):
    print(f'Monitor {i}: {m.width}x{m.height} at ({m.left}, {m.top})')
    print(f'  DPI: {m.dpiScale}, Primary: {m.isPrimary}')

# Auto-position window on second monitor
if len(monitors) > 1:
    m = monitors[1]
    win = op('/project1/window2')
    win.par.winoffsetx = m.left
    win.par.winoffsety = m.top
    win.par.winw = m.width
    win.par.winh = m.height

sections.title: Content Splitting for Wide Canvases
sections.content:
For seamless content spanning multiple screens, render to a single wide canvas and split it per display. Create your content at the combined resolution (e.g., 3840x1080 for two HD screens), then use **Crop TOPs** to extract each display's portion.

This ensures content flows seamlessly across screen boundaries without alignment issues.
sections.code:
# Create a wide canvas and split for two screens
# Assume content TOP is 3840x1080

# Left half for display 1
crop_left = op('/project1/crop_left')
crop_left.par.cropleft = 0
crop_left.par.cropright = 1920
crop_left.par.croptop = 0
crop_left.par.cropbottom = 1080

# Right half for display 2
crop_right = op('/project1/crop_right')
crop_right.par.cropleft = 1920
crop_right.par.cropright = 3840
crop_right.par.croptop = 0
crop_right.par.cropbottom = 1080

sections.title: Open and Manage Windows
sections.content:
Use `window.par.winopen` to programmatically open and close output windows. For installations, you typically open all windows on startup using an **Execute DAT** with the `Start` option enabled.

During development, toggle windows with keyboard shortcuts by using a **Keyboard In DAT** connected to a script that pulses the open parameter.
